topic: New flagship topic: proximal difference-in-differences bridge frontier with negative controls. Pre-anchor check: closest published anchors are Miao-Geng-Tchetgen proximal causal inference, Tchetgen-Tchetgen negative-control bridge work, standard two-period and staggered DiD identification, and recent papers on difference-in-differences with negative controls. Our theorem is not those because the focal object is a finite pretrend-proxy bridge operator that sharply separates three regimes: ordinary parallel-trends DiD identifies ATT, proximal negative-control DiD identifies ATT even when parallel trends fails, and neither identifies ATT. Require a concrete nonroutine object: an explicit 2-group x 3-period x binary-proxy witness with observed negative-control outcome and exposure, a bridge-rank condition computed from pre-period moments, and a non-equivalence theorem showing the proximal bridge and parallel-trends restrictions are neither nested nor equivalent. If the proposal reduces to generic proximal completeness, ordinary DiD placebo tests, support coverage, or a definition-unfold rank condition without a worked witness and non-equivalence class, pivot or stop early.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize the finite 2-group x 3-period binary-proxy bridge-rank frontier under which proximal negative-control DiD identifies ATT after ordinary parallel trends fails, and under which neither restriction identifies ATT.

source_refs.claim: Identifies causal effects with at least two independent proxy variables satisfying a rank condition; when proxy rank is unmet, the paper only gives limited testing rather than full identification.
source_refs.author: Miao, Geng, and Tchetgen Tchetgen

source_refs.claim: Interprets standard DiD as a negative-outcome-control strategy under equal unobserved-confounder association across exposure groups and time.
source_refs.author: Sofer, Richardson, Colicino, Schwartz, and Tchetgen Tchetgen

source_refs.claim: Contrasts COCA, DiD, generalized DiD, and double-negative-control approaches and shows that cardinality and negative-control-validity violations can bias full-identification formulas.
source_refs.author: Penning de Vries and Groenwold

source_refs.claim: Proposes a semiparametric doubly robust DiD estimator using a pair of negative controls for omitted-covariate bias, but does not state a finite preperiod binary-proxy rank frontier.
source_refs.author: Gregori
source_refs.venue: Proceedings of the European Marketing Academy

source_refs.excerpt: Add finite examples exhibiting all three phases with explicit endpoint LP certificates
No sharp finite fallback set when the bridge operator is rank deficient.

open_problems.why_unsolved: The web literature supplies the two poles but not the finite frontier: proximal causal inference gives bridge/rank conditions for point identification, while negative-control DiD gives additive equi-confounding or calibration conditions. The prior proximal-phase review identifies the same missing object at finite support: all three phases need explicit proxy matrices and endpoint certificates, and the rejected angle notes the absence of a sharp fallback set when the bridge operator is rank deficient.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to apply the generic Miao-Geng-Tchetgen proxy rank theorem fails because Sofer et al. identify DiD through an equi-confounding restriction rather than a finite pretrend bridge, and the prior reviewer says the missing object is a finite sharp bridge-envelope realization with explicit endpoint certificates.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sufficient-and-necessary finite-dimensional identification theorem with an explicit 2-group x 3-period x binary-proxy witness, a preperiod bridge-rank matrix computed from observed moments, and sharp examples for parallel-trends-only, proximal-only, and nonidentified regimes.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize an observable equivalence frontier between native proximal OR/IPW/DR bridge objectives and finite minimax negative-control bridge objectives, with strict non-equivalence off the frontier.

source_refs.excerpt: actual estimator-frontier theorem between named Cui proximal OR/IPW/DR and Kallus-Mao-Uehara minimax estimators
a computable threshold that sharply separates asymptotic equivalence and strict non-equivalence

open_problems.why_unsolved: The prior proximal-phase angle was downgraded because a shared exact-fiber quadratic reduction made the proposed workflow frontier look like generic finite convex algebra. Reviewers asked instead for the native population objectives of the named proximal estimators and a threshold computable before assuming both reduce to the same selector.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to put both bridge solvers on a shared exact-fiber quadratic selector fails because the reviewer said this assumption turns the named-workflow frontier into generic finite convex/linear algebra rather than a native estimator equivalence theorem.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: An iff theorem defining the native finite-population losses for named proximal OR/IPW/DR and minimax bridge workflows, an observable threshold, and a finite witness class where equality holds on one side and the selected ATT differs on the other.

open_problems.open_problem: Construct a sharp non-equivalence theorem separating additive parallel trends, odds-ratio equi-confounding, single-proxy negative-control calibration, and double-negative-control proximal bridge restrictions for finite-panel ATT.

source_refs.claim: Argues ordinary parallel trends can be noncredible for binary, count, or polytomous outcomes and replaces it with odds-ratio equi-confounding.
source_refs.author: Tchetgen Tchetgen, Park, and Richardson

source_refs.claim: Gives nonparametric ATT identification with a single negative-control outcome proxy and contrasts that target with two-proxy proximal identification.
source_refs.author: Park, Richardson, and Tchetgen Tchetgen

source_refs.claim: Frames DiD as negative-outcome-control identification under constant additive unobserved-confounder associations.
source_refs.author: Sofer, Richardson, Colicino, Schwartz, and Tchetgen Tchetgen

source_refs.claim: Reviews COCA, DiD, generalized DiD, and double-negative-control approaches and emphasizes that each relies on distinct strong assumptions.
source_refs.author: Penning de Vries and Groenwold

open_problems.why_unsolved: Recent negative-control DiD work has produced several alternative assumptions, but the sources compare them conceptually rather than giving finite observational distributions proving that none of additive parallel trends, odds-ratio equi-confounding, single-proxy calibration, and double-proxy bridge restrictions nests the others.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to treat these restrictions as scale changes of the same DiD idea fails because Tchetgen-Park-Richardson explicitly replace additive parallel trends with odds-ratio equi-confounding, while Park-Richardson-Tchetgen distinguish single-proxy ATT identification from two-proxy proximal identification.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A finite counterexample table or family proving pairwise non-nesting, with observed-data equality where possible and ATT divergence where identification fails.

open_problems.open_problem: Derive sharp ATT bounds for negative-control DiD when the binary-proxy bridge-rank condition fails but preperiod negative-control moments still restrict unmeasured trend bias.

source_refs.claim: Suggests replacing strong exact-identification conditions with weaker, more verifiable conditions that may yield only partial identification.
source_refs.author: Penning de Vries and Groenwold

source_refs.claim: When only one proxy is available or the rank condition is not met, the paper develops a test of the null hypothesis of no causal effect rather than full identification.
source_refs.author: Miao, Geng, and Tchetgen Tchetgen

source_refs.claim: Conducts robust DiD inference by imposing restrictions on how post-treatment violations can differ from observed pretrend differences.
source_refs.author: Rambachan and Roth

open_problems.why_unsolved: The proximal papers identify under rank/completeness, while DiD sensitivity work bounds effects under restrictions on trend violations. The missing bridge is a sharp identified set that uses failed finite proxy rank and observed negative-control pre-moments as the restriction, rather than abandoning the design or imposing an external smoothness budget.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to declare the ATT unidentified once rank fails because Miao-Geng-Tchetgen only offer null testing fails because Rambachan-Roth and Penning de Vries-Groenwold show that weakened restrictions can still produce informative partial-identification statements.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sharp interval formula for ATT as a function of finite observed DiD and negative-control moment tables, with endpoint sharpness proved by latent completions.

open_problems.open_problem: Determine when multiple pre-treatment periods can be turned into a finite proximal bridge operator for DiD, rather than only a pretrend test, double-DID moment, or synthetic-control fit.

source_refs.claim: Shows multiple pretreatment periods can assess parallel trends, improve accuracy, and allow weaker trend assumptions through double DID and K-DID.
source_refs.author: Egami and Yamauchi

source_refs.claim: Interprets TWFE as a special bridge case and constructs more complex bridge functions for linear factor panel models without requiring time periods to grow infinitely.
source_refs.author: Imbens, Kallus, and Mao

source_refs.claim: Uses proximal inference and surrogates in synthetic-control settings where short pre-intervention periods or long post-intervention periods challenge standard SCM.
source_refs.author: Liu, Tchetgen Tchetgen, and Varjao

open_problems.why_unsolved: Multiple-preperiod DiD work treats preperiods as trend tests or GMM moments, and proximal panel/synthetic-control work treats bridges in factor or surrogate frameworks. The sources do not identify when a minimal two-preperiod table with observed negative-control proxies itself supplies a bridge-rank operator for ATT.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to reuse double DID or K-DID moments fails because Egami-Yamauchi's assumptions are trend restrictions, whereas Imbens-Kallus-Mao and Liu-Tchetgen-Varjao require bridge or surrogate structure not encoded by ordinary pretrend equality alone.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sufficient-and-necessary rank theorem mapping two preperiod proxy-moment arrays to an ATT bridge formula, with explicit comparison to double-DID and proximal synthetic-control moment equations.

literature_map:
1. Miao, Geng, and Tchetgen Tchetgen (2018, Biometrika) is the foundational proxy-identification anchor: two independent proxies plus a rank condition can identify causal effects under unmeasured confounding.
2. Sofer, Richardson, Colicino, Schwartz, and Tchetgen Tchetgen (2016, Statistical Science) connects ordinary DiD to negative-outcome controls under an additive equi-confounding or constant-association condition.
3. Tchetgen Tchetgen, Ying, Cui, Shi, and Miao (2024, Statistical Science) gives the broader proximal causal inference bridge framework for negative-control proxies.
4. Cui, Pu, Shi, Miao, and Tchetgen Tchetgen (2024, JASA) develops proximal ATE and ATT efficiency theory under bridge and completeness conditions.
5. Penning de Vries and Groenwold (2023, Statistical Methods in Medical Research) is a caveat anchor: COCA, DiD, generalized DiD, and double-negative-control methods have distinct assumptions and can fail under cardinality or validity violations.
6. Gregori (2025, EMAC Proceedings) is the closest explicit DiD-with-negative-controls anchor, proposing a semiparametric doubly robust estimator using NCO/NCE pairs for omitted-covariate bias under staggered adoption.
7. Zhang et al. (2025, npj Digital Medicine) develops negative-control-calibrated DiD for real-world data, using NCO estimates for bias calibration and parallel-trend diagnostics.
8. Park, Richardson, and Tchetgen Tchetgen (2024, Biometrics) gives single-proxy control for ATT and explicitly contrasts single-NCO identification with two-proxy proximal identification.
9. Tchetgen Tchetgen, Park, and Richardson (2024, Epidemiology) develops universal DiD under odds-ratio equi-confounding, highlighting scale fragility of additive parallel trends for noncontinuous outcomes.
10. Rambachan and Roth (2023, Review of Economic Studies) supplies the main partial-identification and sensitivity anchor for DiD under bounded post-treatment trend violations informed by pretrends.
11. Egami and Yamauchi (2023, Political Analysis) shows multiple pretreatment periods can support testing, efficiency, and weaker trends-in-trends assumptions through double DID and K-DID.
12. Imbens, Kallus, and Mao (2021, arXiv) treats TWFE as a special bridge case in panel factor models and motivates minimal bridge functions without large-T asymptotics.
13. Liu, Tchetgen Tchetgen, and Varjao (2024, AISTATS/PMLR) brings proximal reasoning into synthetic-control settings with surrogates, especially when pre-period information is short.
14. The live gap is not generic proximal completeness or ordinary placebo testing; it is the finite preperiod-proxy bridge frontier separating ordinary parallel-trends DiD, proximal negative-control DiD, and nonidentification.
prior_proposal_map:
Mined active research/eid_proximal_did_bridge_frontier: only state and pipeline initialization existed; no prior proposal/reviewer file for this qid was available.
Mined proximal negative-control banks: eid_proximal_phase_v1 was the strongest adjacent source, with reviewer strengthening_paths demanding finite proxy matrices, LP endpoint certificates, and native estimator-frontier thresholds.
Mined eid_proximal_phase_v1 rejected angle files: the most relevant excerpt states that there is no sharp finite fallback set when the bridge operator is rank deficient.
Mined DiD/pretrend banks: panel_honest_did_phase_v1, pid_tv_pretrend_atet_v1, and pid_did_anticipation_bounded_v1 supplied aggregation, pretrend sensitivity, bounded anticipation, and shared-coordinate support-function gaps.
Mined DID estimator frontier bank q_did_drdid_ipwdid_frontier_v1; reviewer JSONs prescribe a full influence-function or efficiency frontier rather than a beta-score block.
No negative-control DiD bridge proposal exactly matching the new qid was found; the useful prior substrate is the combination of finite proximal-bridge failures with panel/pretrend frontier failures.
Bank READMEs often contain TODO placeholders, so reviewer JSONs and honest-scope flags were higher-yield than README epitaphs.
